title: Calibration of Taiji-2: A Comprehensive Analysis
abstract: We present a comprehensive calibration analysis for the Taiji-2 mission, focusing on laser interferometry and drag-free control systems. Our results demonstrate the feasibility of achieving the required sensitivity for detecting gravitational waves in the millihertz frequency band.

title: GPU-Accelerated Parameter Estimation for Space-Based Gravitational Wave Detectors
authors: Niu Jianwei; Feng Yikun; Zhao Wen
keywords: data analysis; parameter estimation; GPU computing; Bayesian methods
abstract: We introduce pespace, a GPU-accelerated framework for parameter estimation of gravitational wave signals detected by space-based observatories. The framework achieves significant speedup compared to traditional CPU-based methods, enabling rapid analysis of massive black hole binary signals.

title: Detection and Characterization of Verification Binaries for Taiji
authors: Yu Shenghua; Wang Chen; Liu Jian
keywords: verification binaries; white dwarfs; data analysis; mission design
abstract: We present a comprehensive study of verification binaries that will serve as calibration sources for the Taiji mission. Our analysis identifies 15 high-confidence systems with well-characterized orbital parameters, providing crucial benchmarks for validating Taiji's detection capabilities.

title: Three-Band Dark-Siren Cosmology with Taiji, LISA, and Ground-Based Detectors
authors: Song Yijie; Chen Ziming; Fan Xilong
keywords: cosmology; dark-siren; multi-band observations; Hubble constant
abstract: We investigate the potential of three-band gravitational wave observations combining Taiji, LISA, and ground-based detectors for dark-siren cosmology. Our analysis demonstrates that this multi-band approach can constrain the Hubble constant to sub-percent precision within a decade of observations.

title: Time-Delay Interferometry for Taiji: Algorithms and Implementation
keywords: time-delay interferometry; data analysis; laser noise; instrumentation
abstract: We present optimized time-delay interferometry (TDI) algorithms specifically designed for the Taiji mission configuration. Our implementation accounts for the unique orbital characteristics and arm-length variations of Taiji, achieving laser noise suppression of more than 8 orders of magnitude.
